FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 and Year 3 follow-up, N=9799, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, ages 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis of ABCD data using linear and logistic regression to examine cyberbullying victimization (Year 2) as predictor of mental health and substance use outcomes (Year 3).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Lancet Regional Health Americas (2025); no evidence of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The sole exposure variable is cyberbullying victimization (single-item referencing texts, group texts, and social media like Instagram or Snapchat). No separate phone/SM-specific variable (time on phone, SM platform use, SMAQ, MPIQ, app use, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own reported coefficient anywhere in the main tables, supplementary tables, or sensitivity analyses. Screen time appears only as a covariate in a sensitivity analysis, not as IV or DV in a qualifying pairing. Per binding clarification §2.4: cyberbullying alone fails IC2 unless the paper also separately measures a qualifying phone/SM variable with its own coefficient. No such variable exists in this paper. Step 2 not reached. IC2 = NOT_MET.

143.notes: Cyberbullying victimization is the sole exposure. Although the cyberbullying item references social media platforms (Instagram, Snapchat), cyberbullying alone fails IC2 per the binding clarification. Total recreational screen time is included only as a sensitivity covariate adjustment, not as IV or DV with its own coefficient in a qualifying analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Year 3 (2019-2021), 10,305 U.S. early adolescents aged 12-13, 21 recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple regression to examine associations between bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes and sociodemographic predictors in ABCD Year 3 data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Sleep Health (2025); no evidence of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 4 and Table 5 report separate coefficients for phone/SM-specific bedtime modalities paired with individual-trait-level sleep outcomes. Specifically, 'Talk on the phone or text' has OR=1.11 (95% CI 1.05-1.18, p=.001) for sleep disturbance and B=-0.20 (95% CI -0.24 to -0.15, p<.001) for sleep duration; 'Spend time online on social media (e.g., Facebook)' has OR=1.08 (95% CI 1.02-1.15, p=.007) for sleep disturbance and B=-0.25 (95% CI -0.29 to -0.20, p<.001) for sleep duration. Sleep disturbance and sleep duration are individual-trait-level outcomes. Phone/texting and social media are qualifying phone/SM-specific variables. IC2 = MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates reported for phone/SM modalities paired with sleep outcomes: OR, B (regression coefficients), 95% CIs, and p-values for 'Talk on the phone or text' and 'Spend time online on social media' predicting sleep disturbance and sleep duration (Tables 4 and 5).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses reported with numeric results for SM/phone variables paired with individual-trait-level sleep outcomes.

158.notes: The bedtime screen use composite mean score also qualifies under Step 2 (items include phone/text and social media among its components), but Step 1 already returns a hit via the individual phone/text and social media modality-specific coefficients in Tables 4 and 5. FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Year 3 (2019-2022), 10,048 U.S. adolescents aged 12-13, 21 U.S. sites, ABCD 5.0 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Year 3 data using multiple linear regression to assess associations between media parenting practices and adolescent screen time and problematic screen use.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatric Research (2025); no evidence of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports separate regression coefficients for media parenting practices (IV) predicting problematic social media use (SMAQ; DV) and problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ; DV). For example, parental bedroom screen use predicts SMAQ: B=1.91 (95% CI 1.71-2.11, p<0.001); parental limiting of screen time predicts SMAQ: B=-1.58 (95% CI -1.83 to -1.34, p<0.001); parental bedroom screen use predicts MPIQ: B=2.91 (95% CI 2.56-3.26, p<0.001). SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) is a qualifying phone/SM variable; MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) is a qualifying phone variable. Parenting practices are individual-trait-level (family functioning/parenting practices listed as qualifying other-side constructs in criteria §2.4). IC2 = MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric regression coefficients (B) with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 3 for all 6 media parenting practice categories predicting SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ, and total screen time.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses with numeric results for phone/SM variables (SMAQ, MPIQ) paired with individual-trait-level parenting practice variables.

184.FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (ABCD Year 3 cross-sectional data, stated explicitly in methods and limitations). FT-EC7 requires a longitudinal design as condition (a); that condition is not met, so EC7 does not apply.
184.notes: SMAQ and MPIQ are the qualifying phone/SM DVs; parenting practices (family functioning) are the qualifying individual-trait IVs. VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire) is not a qualifying SM/phone variable by itself, but SMAQ and MPIQ independently satisfy IC2. Cross-sectional design confirmed.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data drawn from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, periods 2 and 3 follow-ups (2018–2021), 21 NIH-funded sites, n=9095 adolescents aged 10–13.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: two multivariable linear regression models estimating prospective associations of lifetime and past 12-month cyberbullying with manic symptom sum score 1 year later.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Acta Paediatrica (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Total screen time (hours/day) appears only as a covariate in adjusted models — it is not an IV or DV with a reported coefficient for the SM↔outcome relationship (fails binding clarification: SM as covariate-only). The exposure variables are cyberbullying victimisation (lifetime and past 12-month yes/no). Step 2 not reached. Additionally, cyberbullying alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable under binding clarification #3 (cyberbullying alone fails IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient, which is absent here).

292.notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. Cyberbullying is the IV; manic symptoms is the DV. Screen time included only as a covariate. No phone/SM variable has its own coefficient in any result location.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. ABCD Study Year 3 (2019–2021), N=10,054 adolescents aged 12–13, 21 research sites across the US.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: ordinal logistic regression models assessing associations between six media parenting practice categories and frequency of mature-rated video game use and R-rated movie consumption.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Pediatrics (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable (time on phone, time on SM, named platform, texting, etc.) has its own coefficient in any result location (main tables, appendices D–G). The DVs are R-rated movie consumption frequency and mature-rated video game frequency. Mature/R-rated content alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable (binding clarification #4). The IVs are media parenting practices (parental screen time modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, use of screens to control behavior, parental monitoring, limiting screen time) — these are parenting/contextual variables, not phone/SM variables. Step 2 not reached. No qualifying phone/SM ↔ individual-trait pairing exists anywhere in the paper.

384.notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. DVs are content-rating categories (R-rated movies, mature video games), not qualifying phone/SM variables. IVs are parenting practices. No phone/SM-specific estimate paired with individual-trait variable exists anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. ABCD Study across four annual assessments (baseline 2016–2018 to Year 3 2019–2021), N=9,519, 21 study sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: compositional data analysis (CoDA) with isometric log-ratio transformations and mixed-effects models examining longitudinal changes in screen time subtypes (TV, video games, digital socializing), sleep, and sports/exercise activity over four annual assessments.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Pediatrics (2026 online); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Digital socializing (texting + video chatting + social networking) has its own coefficient estimates (e.g., increased by 0.98 h/day from baseline to Year 3, 95% CI 0.51–1.45, p<0.001; sex-stratified: females 1.39 h/day, males 0.48 h/day). This is a qualifying phone/SM variable. However, the other side of the equation is time/wave (year) and COVID period as the IV in a growth-curve model — these are contextual/temporal variables, not individual-trait-level variables. Sex-stratified analyses use sex as the stratifying variable, which is demographic. No individual-trait-level variable (health outcome, behavioral outcome, cognitive measure, well-being, etc.) appears on the other side of any analysis involving digital socializing or any other phone/SM variable. The paper describes descriptive longitudinal trajectories only; no analysis pairs a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait-level outcome or predictor. Other-side rule: all predictors are time, age (time-varying covariate), COVID period, and baseline demographics — none qualify as individual-trait.

385.notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. Paper describes longitudinal changes in screen time subtypes (including digital socializing, which qualifies as a phone/SM variable) but the only predictor is time/wave (contextual) and demographics — no individual-trait variable on the other side. The study does not pair a phone/SM variable with any individual-trait outcome or predictor.
